🚨 TRUMP IS PREPARING TO FIRE KRISTI NOEM

Prediction markets are rapidly pricing in the possibility that Kristi Noem could soon be out as Secretary of Homeland Security. Odds surged to about 79% that she will leave the post before July 1.

The spike comes as reports say President Donald Trump is weighing her dismissal and has already begun asking aides and Republican lawmakers for potential replacements.

title:
TRUMP PLANS U.S. CONTROL OVER GLOBAL AI CHIP SALES

The Trump administration is drafting rules that would require U.S. approval for nearly all AI chip exports, giving Washington sweeping power over companies like Nvidia and AMD. The draft framework sets licensing rules based on shipment size, from simplified reviews for small orders to government-level approval for massive deployments, potentially tying exports to security guarantees or U.S. investment.

Officials say the goal is to make American AI the global standard while controlling critical infrastructure, though delays or strict conditions could disrupt international AI projects.

title:
IRAN SWAPS MISSILES FOR DRONES IN GULF STRIKES

Iran is increasingly using drones instead of missiles in attacks on Gulf states. UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait report most recent strikes involve Iranian-made Shahed drones, while missile launches have dropped sharply.

Drones carry lighter payloads but are cheaper and easier to mass-launch, posing persistent challenges for air defenses. The shift may reflect U.S. and Israeli targeting of Iran’s missile stockpiles and Tehran’s effort to conserve advanced weapons while maintaining regional pressure.

UAE has intercepted the most projectiles, and Bahrain notes drones now dominate attack waves, highlighting the cost imbalance between cheap Iranian drones and expensive defense systems.

title:
DOLLAR SURGES ON INFLATION FEARS, MIDEAST CONFLICT IMPACT

The dollar climbs as Middle East tensions stoke inflation concerns. Fuel disruptions force regional aluminum plants to halt output, tightening energy supply. CME’s FedWatch shows just a 36% chance of more than one U.S. rate cut this year, up from 22% last week. The WSJ Dollar Index gains 0.3%, with the dollar up 0.3% vs. the euro and 0.4% vs. the yen.

title:
TRUMP HAS LIMITED TOOLS TO COOL ENERGY PRICES

Wolfe Research says the Donald Trump administration has few policy options to curb rising energy prices tied to tensions with Iran.

One proposal is a government-backed maritime insurance program to encourage oil tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, using the authority of the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation.

Other possible steps include releasing oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve, adjusting biofuel rules, or a temporary gasoline tax holiday—though that would require Congress.

Still, analysts say energy prices will depend more on the conflict itself than on policy action.
summary:
TRUMP HAS LIMITED TOOLS TO COOL ENERGY PRICES

Wolfe Research says the Donald Trump administration has few policy options to curb rising energy prices tied to tensions with Iran.

One proposal is a government-backed maritime insurance program to encourage oil tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, using the authority of the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation.

Other possible steps include releasing oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve, adjusting biofuel rules, or a temporary gasoline tax holiday—though that would require Congress.

Still, analysts say energy prices will depend more on the conflict itself than on policy action.

title:
OIL SPIKE IS THE REAL RISK FOR U.S. ECONOMY, BOFA SAYS

The conflict involving the U.S. and Iran hasn’t significantly changed the economic outlook, according to Bank of America.

Analyst Meghan Swiber said risks to the U.S. economy remain limited unless oil prices surge sharply.

A common estimate suggests a $10 rise in crude could lift inflation by about 0.1 percentage points and trim GDP growth by a similar amount. The impact usually fades within a year as higher energy costs curb demand.

The U.S. is also less exposed to oil shocks than in the past because it is now a net exporter of oil and gas.

For now, the Federal Reserve is likely to stay cautious and wait to see whether higher oil prices feed into broader inflation.
